input_dict.agent_role: Summarize first-hop evidence and expose the bridge entity, missing fact, or retrieval direction needed for the next hop.
input_dict.question: What company published a fantasy tabletop role-playing game originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson that has a book of rules that lack the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game?
input_dict.current_output:
- Passage facts: "Dungeons & Dragons" is the fantasy tabletop role‑playing game "originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson." The Player's Handbook is "a book of rules for the fantasy role-playing game 'Dungeons & Dragons'" and "does not contain the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game."  
- Bridge / missing verification: identify which company published that game (i.e., the publisher of Dungeons & Dragons). Passages contain two publisher attributions that the next hop should confirm/choose between: the game was "first published ... by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR)" and the game "has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro)".  
- Retrieval direction / anchors: search for publisher attribution for the Dungeons & Dragons original/Player's Handbook edition using anchors: "originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson," "Player's Handbook ... does not contain the complete set of rules," "first published ... by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR)," and "has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro)."

upstream_context.question: What company published a fantasy tabletop role-playing game originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson that has a book of rules that lack the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game?
upstream_context.hop1_query: What company published a fantasy tabletop role-playing game originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson that has a book of rules that lack the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game?
hop1_docs: Dungeons & Dragons | Dungeons & Dragons (abbreviated as D&D or DnD) is a fantasy tabletop role-playing game (RPG) originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson, and first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR).  The game has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro) since 1997.  It was derived from miniature wargames with a variation of the "Chainmail" game serving as the initial rule system.  "D&D"'s publication is commonly recognized as the beginning of modern role-playing games and the role-playing game industry.

Dungeons & Dragons (1974) | The original Dungeons & Dragons (commonly abbreviated "D&D") boxed set by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson was published by TSR, Inc. in 1974.  It initially included the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.  Its product designation was TSR 2002.

Player's Handbook | The Player's Handbook (Players Handbook in first edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" ("AD&D")) is a book of rules for the fantasy role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D").  It does not contain the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game.  Additional rules, for use by Dungeon Masters (DMs), who referee the game, can be found in the "Dungeon Master's Guide".  Many optional rules, such as those governing extremely high-level players, and some of the more obscure spells, are found in other sources.

Don't Give Up the Ship! | Don't Give Up the Ship is a set of rules for conducting Napoleonic era naval wargames.  The game was published by Guidon Games in 1972 and republished by TSR, Inc. in 1975.  It was the first collaboration between Dave Arneson and Gary Gygax, the co-creators of "Dungeons & Dragons".  "D&D" fans may also recognize the name of contributor Mike Carr, who edited the rules and researched the historical single ship actions that are included as game scenarios.

Gary Gygax | Ernest Gary Gygax ( ) (July 27, 1938 – March 4, 2008) was an American game designer and author best known for co-creating the pioneering role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") with Dave Arneson.  Gygax has been described as the father of "D&D".

Blackmoor (supplement) | Blackmoor is a supplementary rulebook (product designation TSR 2004) of the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game written by Dave Arneson (with a foreword by Gary Gygax).

Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set | The Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set is a set of rulebooks for the "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") fantasy role-playing game.  First published in 1977, it saw a handful of revisions and reprintings.  The first edition was written by J. Eric Holmes based on Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson's original work.  Later editions were edited by Tom Moldvay, Dave Cook, and Frank Mentzer.

visible_gold_support.title: Player's Handbook
visible_gold_support.sentence: It does not contain the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game.

You are generating a gold-conditioned destination output for one agent
inside a multi-agent question-answering pipeline.

The destination is not a prompt update. It is an ideal output that remains
possible from the selected agent's actual input boundary.

Use only:
- the supplied task and upstream context;
- gold supporting sentences explicitly listed as visible_gold_support;
- missing support titles only as retrieval targets.

Do not infer or state the hidden contents of a missing support document.
Do not use or guess the gold answer. Preserve the functional role of the
selected agent.

Agent:
summary1

Agent role:
Summarize first-hop evidence and expose the bridge entity, missing fact, or retrieval direction needed for the next hop.

Destination requirements:
Produce an ideal summary1 output using only information available at the
summary1 boundary.

The output should:
- summarize useful evidence from the actual first-hop retrieval;
- identify the bridge entity, relation, or missing evidence needed next;
- expose a compact retrieval direction for the query agent;
- remain a summary1 output rather than a final answer;
- not state hidden second-hop facts or the gold answer;
- treat missing support titles only as retrieval targets, not as evidence
  that their unseen documents contain a particular answer fact.

  "destination_output": "Summary of first-hop evidence:
- Dungeons & Dragons is a fantasy tabletop RPG originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson; the first-hop text explicitly says it was first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR).
- The Player's Handbook is described as a rules book for Dungeons & Dragons that does not contain the complete rules and includes only rules for use by players.

Bridge / missing element for next hop:
- The needed bridge is the publisher (company) attribution that connects the cited Player's Handbook instance to the game's publisher — i.e., confirm which company published the Dungeons & Dragons edition associated with the Player's Handbook reference (the first-hop evidence names Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR) as the original publisher).

Compact retrieval direction for the query agent:
- Retrieve and confirm authoritative publisher attribution for Dungeons & Dragons (search query examples: "Dungeons & Dragons publisher", "published by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. TSR Dungeons & Dragons", "Player's Handbook publisher edition").
- If multiple publishers/editions are mentioned, retrieve sources that map the Player's Handbook edition in question to its publisher so the final answer can state the correct company.",
  "rationale": "The visible supporting sentences state (1) that Dungeons & Dragons was originally designed by Gygax and Arneson and was first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR), and (2) that the Player's Handbook does not contain the complete rules and only includes rules for players. From these, the missing linking fact is the publisher attribution tying the named game/Player's Handbook to a company; next-hop retrieval should therefore confirm the publisher (company) attribution."
